key_insight: The paper defines a Hausdorff-distance framework on solution sets to prove when governing ODEs can be uniquely identified from observed trajectories and to derive sample-complexity bounds for that recovery task.
engineering_relevance: This provides theory-backed guidance for scientific machine learning systems that infer dynamics from data, indicating how many trajectories may be needed and when model classes are distinguishable.
method: Introduce the Hausdorff distance between solution sets as the comparison metric for ODEs.
Derive identifiability bounds for linear and nonlinear ODE families, including Lipschitz/Hölder vector fields.
Use metric entropy estimates to translate distinguishability results into sample-complexity bounds.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports theoretical analysis only, with identifiability bounds, metric entropy estimates, and sample-complexity results rather than experimental evaluation.
watch_item: The bounds depend on assumptions such as admissible initial conditions and regularity of the vector field, so practical recovery may be harder for noisy or misspecified data.
paper_title: Recovering Governing Equations from Solution Data: Identifiability Bounds for Linear and Nonlinear ODEs
